questions.question: Explain the different types of variables in Java with examples.
questions.hint: Focus on where variables are declared and their scope
keyPoints: Instance variables - declared in class, outside methods, belong to objects
Local variables - declared inside methods or blocks
Class/static variables - shared among all objects, declared with static keyword
Parameters - variables in method declarations
Primitive types vs reference types

questions.question: List and explain the nine implicit objects available in JSP pages.
questions.hint: Remember that exception is only available in error pages with isErrorPage=true
keyPoints: request - HttpServletRequest object for client requests
response - HttpServletResponse object for server response
out - JspWriter object for output stream
session - HttpSession object for user session
application - ServletContext object for application scope
pageContext - PageContext for page-level data
page - Object reference to the servlet instance
config - ServletConfig object for servlet configuration
exception - Throwable object for exception handling (only in error pages)

questions.question: Differentiate between instance variables and local variables in Java.
questions.hint: Think about default values and where they can be accessed
keyPoints: Instance variables are declared in class but outside methods
Local variables are declared inside methods, constructors, or blocks
Instance variables have default values; local variables must be initialized before use
Instance variables are accessible throughout the class; local variables only within declaring block
Instance variables are stored in heap memory; local variables in stack memory
Instance variables have object-level scope; local variables have method-level scope

questions.question: What are cookies in servlets? Explain how to create and retrieve cookies.
questions.hint: Remember cookies are stored on client side and sent with each request
keyPoints: Cookies are small pieces of data stored on client side
Created using new Cookie(name, value) constructor
Added to response using response.addCookie(cookie)
Retrieved using request.getCookies() which returns Cookie array
Cookies have attributes: setMaxAge(), setPath(), setDomain()
Cookies are sent in HTTP headers between client and server

questions.question: Explain variable scope in JSP with respect to different implicit objects.
questions.hint: Scope determines how long and where variables are accessible
keyPoints: Page scope - limited to single page, stored in pageContext
Request scope - available during single request, stored in request attribute
Session scope - available throughout user session, stored in session attribute
Application scope - available to all users across application, stored in servletContext
pageContext.setAttribute() for page scope
request.setAttribute() for request scope
session.setAttribute() for session scope
application.setAttribute() for application scope

questions.question: Explain the difference between request.getParameter() and request.getAttribute() methods.
questions.hint: Think about data source - client input vs server-side objects
keyPoints: getParameter() returns String value of form field or URL parameter
getAttribute() returns Object stored in request scope
getParameter() always returns String or null
getAttribute() returns Object and requires casting
getParameter() for client input data
getAttribute() for server-side data sharing between components

questions.question: Explain the complete lifecycle of a servlet from loading to destruction. How are instance variables managed during this lifecycle?
questions.hint: Focus on thread safety implications of instance variables in servlets
keyPoints: Loading - ClassLoader loads servlet class into memory
Instantiation - Container creates servlet instance using no-arg constructor
Initialization - init() method called once, can override to initialize resources
Request Handling - service() method called for each request, dispatches to doGet/doPost
Destruction - destroy() method called when servlet is unloaded, cleanup resources
Instance variables persist across requests but not thread-safe
Each request runs in separate thread sharing instance variables
Local variables in service methods are thread-safe (stack memory)
Warning: Avoid using instance variables for request-specific data

questions.question: Compare JSP and Servlets with respect to variable declaration, implicit objects, and execution model.
questions.hint: Understand that JSP is translated into servlet internally
keyPoints: Servlets - pure Java classes, explicit out.print() for output
JSP - translated to servlet automatically by container
JSP has 9 implicit objects; servlets have fewer built-in objects
In JSP: instance variables declared in declaration tag <%! %>
In JSP: local variables in scriptlet <% %>
JSP provides easier HTML integration; servlets better for business logic
JSP has page, request, session, application scopes
Servlets require explicit request/session handling
JSP compiles to servlet; lifecycle similar to servlet
JSP expression <%= %> for outputting values

questions.question: Design a complete servlet example that demonstrates reading form parameters, using session attributes, and setting cookies.
questions.hint: Combine all three concepts in a single flow
keyPoints: Extend HttpServlet and override doPost/doGet
Use request.getParameter() to read form fields
Create/update session using request.getSession()
Set session attributes using session.setAttribute(name, value)
Create cookies with new Cookie(name, value)
Set cookie max age using setMaxAge()
Add cookie to response using response.addCookie()
Forward or redirect to another page
Include proper import statements
Handle null checks for parameters

questions.question: Explain the three types of scripting elements in JSP with syntax and examples. How do they differ in terms of where declared code executes?
questions.hint: Remember <%! %> creates class-level members, <% %> creates method-level code
keyPoints: Declarations <%! %> - declare instance variables and methods, compiled in servlet class
Scriptlets <% %> - contain Java code executed in service method, local variables
Expressions <%= %> - evaluate and print result, converted to out.print()
Declaration example: <%! int count = 0; %>
Scriptlet example: <% for(int i=0; i<10; i++) { %>
Expression example: <%= user.getName() %>
Declarations execute once per servlet instance
Scriptlets execute on each request
Expressions evaluate to String for each request

questions.question: Discuss session tracking techniques in Java web applications. Compare cookies, URL rewriting, and hidden form fields.
questions.hint: Understand when each technique is applicable and its limitations
keyPoints: Session tracking maintains user state across multiple requests
Cookies - stored on client, sent with each request, can be disabled
URL rewriting - append session ID to URLs, works when cookies disabled
Hidden form fields - stored in form, sent with POST, requires form submission
HttpSession - server-side session management, uses cookies or URL rewriting
session.setAttribute() to store data
session.getAttribute() to retrieve data
session.isNew() to check if session is new
session.invalidate() to end session
Each technique has pros/cons regarding security, limitations, and browser support

questions.hint: Trace each line and identify where data is stored and retrieved
keyPoints: request implicit object used - Request scope for getting parameters
session implicit object used - Session scope for user data
application implicit object used - Application scope for shared data
pageContext implicit object used - Page scope for counter variable
out implicit object used implicitly for expression output
request has request scope - available during single request
session has session scope - available across user session
application has application scope - available to all users
pageContext has page scope - only on this page
Variables: name (local), counter (page-scoped via pageContext)
count retrieved from pageContext must be cast to Integer
Output: Welcome [username]! Visitor #[counter value]

questions.question: Write a servlet that counts how many times a user has visited a website using cookies. Show the complete code with proper handling of new vs returning visitors.
questions.hint: Handle the null case when no cookies exist on first visit
keyPoints: Extend HttpServlet, override doGet method
Check for existing cookies using request.getCookies()
Loop through cookies to find "visitCount"
If found: increment count, update cookie
If not found: create new cookie with count=1
Set cookie path to "/" and maxAge for persistence
Set cookie in response using response.addCookie()
Create welcome message based on visit count
Handle null return from getCookies() properly
Use Integer.parseInt() for string to int conversion
Proper imports: javax.servlet.*, javax.servlet.http.*

questions.hint: Instance variables shared across all requests cause race conditions
keyPoints: Line 1: counter is instance variable - NOT thread-safe, shared across requests
Line 2: userData is instance variable - NOT thread-safe, race condition possible
Line 3: input is local variable - thread-safe, each request has own stack
Line 4: Assigning to instance variable - causes race condition with concurrent requests
Line 5: Incrementing counter - race condition, lost updates possible
Fix 1: Use local variables instead of instance variables for request data
Fix 2: Use synchronization (synchronized blocks or methods) - impacts performance
Fix 3: Use ThreadLocal for servlet to maintain separate copy per thread
Fix 4: Use request attributes to pass data between methods
Best practice: Avoid instance variables for request-specific data in servlets

questions.question: What is the difference between forwarding a request and redirecting a response in servlets? How does this affect variable/data handling?
questions.hint: Think about number of requests and data preservation
keyPoints: forward() - server-side transfer, same request object, URL unchanged
sendRedirect() - client-side redirect, new request, URL changes
Forward: Uses RequestDispatcher, maintains request attributes
Redirect: Two separate requests, loses request attributes
Forward: Faster, happens on server
Redirect: Slower, browser makes new request
Forward: Browser shows original URL
Redirect: Browser shows redirected URL
For session attributes: forward maintains, redirect maintains if cookies enabled
For application attributes: both maintain
